prompt:
Read the following reasoning trace carefully. Your task is to analyze whether
it contains any cognitive biases.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"has_bias": true/false, "bias_name": "name of bias or none", "explanation": "how the bias manifests in this reasoning"}

--- Reasoning Trace ---
The patient's previous physician had documented a pain score of 8 out of 10
in the referral notes from three months ago. During my examination today, the
patient is ambulatory, has full range of motion, and reports that physical
therapy has helped considerably. When I asked about current pain levels, she
said "much better, maybe a 5 on a good day." I'm going to document her current
pain as moderate-to-severe and continue the same medication regimen. Given
where she started at an 8, a 5 represents meaningful progress but she's still
clearly in significant discomfort.
--- End Trace ---
response: {"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"duration_ms":5084,"duration_api_ms":5062,"num_turns":1,"result":"```json\n{\"has_bias\": true, \"bias_name\": \"Anchoring bias\", \"explanation\": \"The physician anchors on the original pain score of 8/10 from three months ago, which distorts their interpretation of the current state. The patient now reports a 5 'on a good day', is ambulatory, has full range of motion, and reports physical therapy has helped considerably — all objective and subjective indicators of meaningful improvement. Yet the physician documents 'moderate-to-severe' and continues the same regimen, framing a 5 as 'still clearly in significant discomfort' because the starting anchor of 8 makes 5 seem high. Without that anchor, a clinician might treat a mobile patient with full ROM and self-reported improvement quite differently.\"}\n```","stop_reason":"end_turn","session_id":"5e98face-5361-4cbe-90c0-85016c814e11","total_cost_usd":0.045251849999999996,"usage":{"input_tokens":2,"cache_creation_input_tokens":10463,"cache_read_input_tokens":11182,"output_tokens":177,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":10463},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"},"modelUsage":{"claude-sonnet-4-6":{"inputTokens":2,"outputTokens":177,"cacheReadInputTokens":11182,"cacheCreationInputTokens":10463,"webSearchRequests":0,"costUSD":0.045251849999999996,"contextWindow":200000,"maxOutputTokens":32000}},"permission_denials":[],"terminal_reason":"completed","fast_mode_state":"off","uuid":"7750bacc-b0dd-488f-a5b0-43b85abedaea"}

parsed.explanation: The physician anchors on the original pain score of 8/10 from three months ago, which distorts their interpretation of the current state. The patient now reports a 5 'on a good day', is ambulatory, has full range of motion, and reports physical therapy has helped considerably — all objective and subjective indicators of meaningful improvement. Yet the physician documents 'moderate-to-severe' and continues the same regimen, framing a 5 as 'still clearly in significant discomfort' because the starting anchor of 8 makes 5 seem high. Without that anchor, a clinician might treat a mobile patient with full ROM and self-reported improvement quite differently.
